item.title: Israel's new exhibit exposes years of Hamas terrorist planning before Oct 7 massacre
item.description: A 600-square-meter exhibition features Hamas tunnel replicas, attack vehicles, and documents revealing Hezbollah and Iran's roles in the Oct. 7 assault.
item.content:encoded:
The "Return to October" exhibition offers an immersive reconstruction of the Hamas-led Oct. 7, 2023, massacre, combining video with declassified Israel Defense Forces intelligence and planning materials recovered in the Gaza Strip that reveal how the Palestinian terrorist group planned and carried out its cross-border assault.

The 600-square-meter exhibition, assembled by the Israel Intelligence Heritage & Commemoration Center (IICC), a nonprofit dedicated to commemorating fallen members of Israel's intelligence community.

The exhibition features vehicles used in the attack, a film on the abduction of Israeli hostages, a mock Hamas tunnel, and excerpts from the Israel Defense Forces' 47-minute compilation of raw footage from the assault.

INSIDE ISRAEL'S MISSION TO TRAIN CIVILIANS TO STOP THE NEXT OCT 7-LIKE TERROR ATTACK

"The main conclusion of this exhibition is that Hamas prepared this terror invasion over a long period in a highly organized and professional manner. It gathered extensive intelligence over many years, and visitors can see examples of that intelligence-gathering effort throughout the exhibition," Yossi Kuperwasser, head of the Institute for the Research of the Methodology of Intelligence at the Israel Intelligence Heritage & Commemoration Center, told Fox News Digital.

Kuperwasser said the exhibition's most significant feature is its collection of documents seized by the IDF in Gaza that Hamas sought to keep hidden. Among them are materials indicating that Hezbollah chose not to fully join the Oct. 7 attack out of concern that Israel would respond with a major offensive. The documents also shed light on the relationship between Hamas and Iran, including the extent of Tehran's involvement.

"The most important lesson is that Israel's enemies are constantly planning large-scale terrorist attacks. They seek to wipe us off the map, and if you become complacent and assume they are not preparing such attacks, you are mistaken," he said.

ISRAEL'S MILITARY ELIMINATES HAMAS TERRORIST WHO HELPED ABDUCT AMERICAN-ISRAELI HOSTAGE, 3 OTHERS

The exhibition's first room features a map marking 114 points where terrorists breached the border into Israel on Oct. 7. Red circles identify the communities that were attacked, while a dotted red line traces where the attackers were ultimately stopped. According to the exhibition, about 4,300 Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad and other terrorist operatives entered Israel at the start of the assault, followed by another 1,500 to 1,700 Gazans described as "uninvolved civilians."

Displayed in the room are Egyptian, North Korean, Russian, Chinese and Iranian-made weapons, alongside arms supplied to Hamas by Hezbollah.

The tour guide Eyal, whose last name could not be disclosed, displayed a kidnapping kit containing handcuffs, electric shock devices and syringes intended to sedate hostages, alongside what he described as a "spiritual kit" containing a miniature Quran and a fatwa, or religious ruling, calling for the killing of Israelis.

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION PROBES UNRWA OVER ALLEGED EMPLOYMENT OF 1,500 TERRORISTS

Another display features military gear belonging to former Hamas leader Yahya Sinwar, as well as a handwritten document recovered by the IDF from the European Hospital in Gaza.

"Here, he instructs Hamas terrorists to attack the Zionists, burn villages, kill and kidnap people, humiliate soldiers, and document everything using GoPro cameras for social media," Eyal said.

The document also states that Sinwar hoped Iran, the Houthis, Hezbollah, Arabs in Judea and Samaria, and Arab citizens of Israel would join the assault.

Also on display are aerial photographs that Hamas used to map Kibbutz Be’eri, identifying individual homes, the armory and the security chief’s residence; an operational plan dated Oct. 6 that was shared only with Hamas commanders to prevent leaks; and a booklet translating Arabic phrases into Hebrew.

The exhibition also highlights Hamas education and incitement. Shelves display books recovered from Gaza, including copies of Mein Kampf found in schools, while photographs show children posing with weapons after attending Hamas summer camps.

"It begins in childhood," Eyal told Fox News Digital.

Another room focuses on Hamas’s exploitation of journalists and UNRWA for terrorist purposes, featuring displays that organizers say show 60% of reporters killed in Gaza were Hamas members or affiliated with the organization, a photograph of a UNRWA school allegedly used as a combat headquarters, and a map of Israel labeling towns and cities with Arabic names.

A garage-like room displays a white Toyota pickup truck and motorcycles used in the terror assault.

"We found that 153 pickup trucks and 350 motorcycles entered Israel that day. Many of these Toyotas were imported into Gaza through Ashdod Port in Israel," Eyal said.

Another room shows excerpts from the IDF’s 47-minute compilation of raw footage from the attacks. Many journalists chose not to watch because of the graphic content, while some who remained in the room left in tears.

Toward the end, a corridor displays the names and photographs, in alphabetical order, of the 1,224 people killed during the attacks, including soldiers, police officers, civilians, Jews, Arabs, Christians, and Druze.

"When we fail in our mission, this is the situation we arrive at," Eyal said.

The final room features footage of Israeli hostages reuniting with their families and concludes with an optimistic painting depicting a flourishing southern Israel.

item.title: GOP senators unveil move to memorialize communism victims on CCP's doorstep amid Dem civil war: 'No tolerance'
item.description: The proposed memorial would sit across from the Chinese Embassy, forcing CCP officials to face victims of Mao Zedong's rule and the Uyghur genocide daily.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Sens. Rick Scott, R-Fla., and Marsha Blackburn, R-Tenn., introduced legislation Tuesday to create a memorial in Washington, D.C., honoring victims of genocide, crimes against humanity and political persecution committed by the Chinese Communist Party as the Democratic Party deals with a socialist civil war in its ranks.

The bill, titled the Honoring the Victims of Communist China's Tyranny Act, would require the State Department to establish a memorial in International Park, across from the CCP Embassy, honoring victims of the Chinese Communist Party's persecution. The memorial would commemorate victims from Mao Zedong's rule, whose policies historians estimate killed tens of millions of people, to the Tiananmen Square crackdown and the genocide against Uyghurs recognized by the U.S. government.

"The people of China deserve nothing less than the God-given liberty and unalienable rights America's founders fought for 250 years ago," Scott said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"America should have zero tolerance for Communist China and its crimes.

REPORT DETAILS RISING PRESSURE ON UNDERGROUND CATHOLICS AS CHINA DENIES CRACKDOWN

Scott added Xi Jinping and the CCP are "responsible for some of the worst human rights abuses in history," citing forced slave labor, torture, forced sterilizations and organ harvesting as well as several "other horrors."

In an X post on Tuesday, Scott criticized far-left political influencer Hasan Piker, who previously praised Zedong as "one of the great leaders of this world" and "a man who changed the entire universe, the entire planet." Piker, who has roughly 10 million followers across social media, has also drawn criticism for past inflammatory remarks, including saying the United States "deserved" 9/11, and recently campaigned with Democratic Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed in Michigan.

HASAN PIKER VOWS NEVER TO CRITICIZE MAO ZEDONG IN RESURFACED VIDEO, HAILS HIM AS ‘GREAT LEADER’

"Mao Zedong's dictatorship of the CCP resulted in the deaths of tens of MILLIONS of people," Scott wrote.

"We know Hasan Piker shills for communists, but praising a dictator who is known for mass murder & causing one of the worst famines in human history is beyond the pale. This is the new face of the Democrat Party."

Blackburn echoed that sentiment, telling Fox News Digital that the CCP "is guilty of the worst human rights abuses imaginable."

"Our legislation would honor victims of Communist China's tyranny by allocating existing funds and donations to build a memorial that will ensure America never forgets the human cost of the CCP's oppression," she said.

MORNING GLORY: WILL XI FREE LAI?

Citing jailed Hong Kong publisher and democracy activist Jimmy Lai as an example, Scott said anyone who speaks out against the Chinese government will "pay the price." Lai is serving a 20-year prison sentence under Hong Kong's national security law, which Secretary of State Marco Rubio has called "unjust and tragic."

By placing the memorial across from the CCP Embassy, Scott said Chinese officials would be reminded daily of the CCP's crimes.

"As Xi's henchmen at the embassy walk into work every day, they should have to face the litany of crimes in which they've been complicit," he said.

The bill would require the memorial to express "solidarity and friendship of the people of the United States with the people of the People's Republic of China" and "the hope and prayer that the Chinese people may one day enjoy the blessings of liberty."

The memorial would include exhibits documenting "the genocide of Uyghurs and other ethnic and religious minorities in Xinjiang," as well as efforts to suppress the culture, religion, history and language of Tibetans and Southern Mongolians, according to the bill.

The legislation limits taxpayer funding for the memorial to $500,000 while permitting private donations to fund the remainder.

item.title: Trump administration has lost or abandoned hundreds of lawsuits against anti-ICE protesters, migrants: report
item.description: A report said the Trump administration lost or abandoned hundreds of cases charging anti-ICE protesters and migrants with assaulting agents.
item.content:encoded:
The Trump administration has lost or abandoned hundreds of cases in which it accused anti-ICE protesters and migrants of assaulting federal immigration agents, a report said.

An analysis from The New York Times showed that of the 558 people charged, 191 cases were dismissed, and 22 people were acquitted. Only four were convicted after trial, while 246 pleaded guilty and another 95 cases are pending.

The newspaper reported the Justice Department typically has a record of more than 90% of defendants pleading guilty or being convicted at trial. The Trump administration’s strategy in court has been leaning on 18 U.S.C. 111, a statute that makes it a federal crime to assault or forcibly impede a government officer and carries punishments ranging from a fine to 20 years in prison, it added.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Department of Homeland Security and the Justice Department for comment.

NEW YORK TIMES TORCHED FOR 'FALSE' REPORTING ON ICE INVESTIGATIONS, OUTLET FIRES BACK

DHS spokeswoman Lauren Bis told The New York Times that "it should come as no surprise that there’s an increase in criminal referrals under 18 U.S.C. 111 as there’s been a massive increase in violence and threats against federal law enforcement."

"Federal prosecutors are correct to prioritize these prosecutions and hold individuals accountable to the fullest extent of the law," added Justice Department spokesman Wyn Hornbuckle, describing how prosecutors, at times, reduced or dropped charges based on "mitigating factors identified in a case."

The New York Times cited another Justice Department official as saying that prosecutors in some of the cases dropped charges when the defendants were deported from the United States.

The newspaper said it based its analysis on court filings, hearing transcripts, videos of encounters and interviews with witnesses and federal officials.

ICE RESUMES NATIONWIDE TRAFFIC STOPS UNDER NEW POLICY REQUIRING BODY CAMERAS

It reported that in dozens of the cases that were lost or abandoned, court records and video showed that federal agents initiated physical struggles, such as shoving, tackling and pepper spraying defendants. Other defendants were able to successfully argue that the assaults they were accused of committing against the federal agents were acts of self-defense, the newspaper added.

Two of the judges overseeing the cases discovered agents purposely destroyed evidence, including demanding that a defendant delete images from their cellphone, according to The New York Times.

Officers also charged more than two dozen people who allegedly filmed or followed agents, sometimes honking car horns or shouting phrases like, "La migra is coming!," despite no allegations of physical contact with agents, the newspaper reported.

In one case, a judge dismissed assault charges against an immigrant after ruling that the agent involved was cut by glass from a car window that the agent had smashed, and in 65 instances, prosecutors abandoned or dropped charges before deadlines to present evidence to a grand jury or judge, the New York Times also said.

The report did note one occasion when federal immigration officers were seriously hurt by protesters and migrants, including the dragging of an officer who had his arm inside a car window.

item.title: ESPN laying off Karl Ravech leaves fans fuming, but the writing has been on the wall for years
item.description: Karl Ravech's shocking departure from ESPN stunned fans who knew him as the beloved voice of Sunday Night Baseball and the College World Series.
item.content:encoded:
What a week over at ESPN. It looks a little different today, if you haven't heard.

The World Wide Leader laid off dozens of employees this week, starting with Monday's on-air debacle with Ryan Clark. OutKick broke that story, by the way, despite what the PR folks at ESPN want to tell you.

Anyway, that's neither here nor there.

Clark was let go, along with Cam Newton and Tom Pelissero, among others. Clark was the biggest name because he was the biggest gaslighter, but the other two were somewhat stunning, too.

Newton had become a fixture on First Take, while Pelissero is well-respected — although he was more of a casualty of the merger with NFL Network. A lot of cooks in the NFL insider kitchen now.

The biggest stunner, however? How about ESPN deciding to cut ties with longtime baseball voice, Karl Ravech? That one shocked the internet.

Furthermore, it infuriated the internet. Everyone was pretty mixed on Clark. You either loved him or hated him.

But Karl Ravech? The 33-year vet? The voice of Sunday Night Baseball? The voice of Baseball Tonight? The voice of the College World Series?

That one upset people:

I could go on and on. It's a war zone over on Elon's app right now, and rightfully so.

Ravech, by the way, was also the voice of the Little League World Series, which is set to start next month. Brutal move.

Look, layoffs are all part of the job, especially in this business. I've only been in it for 10 years, but I've seen at least four rounds of layoffs already. Never been part of one (mainly because I don't get paid enough), but I assume I will someday.

I remember years ago I was out covering a Stetson baseball tournament game when a massive round of layoffs hit the office, particularly the sports department. I came back that afternoon and we had lost two sports editors and a writer. Doesn't sound like a lot, but that was half our staff at that point.

I left in the morning and it was a regular day, and came back to three empty chairs. It was a bloodbath. Wild.

Anyway, firing Karl Ravech is a gut-punch to anyone who grew up watching baseball and ESPN. Remember the glory days of Ravech, Peter Gammons, Harold Reynolds and Steve Bethiaume?

Man, we had it all:

Lordy. What a time to be alive. I miss it so much.

Anyone whose been halfway paying attention knew this was coming, by the way. ESPN put all of its eggs in the NBA basket years ago, for some asinine reason.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

ESPN also continue to distance itself from MLB, evidenced by the nasty fallout between the two last year that resulted in Rob Manfred shopping around the TV rights. That really got the ball rolling in terms of cutting the cord, and this latest move is the next logical step.

As for Ravech, I'd imagine he'll land on his feet pretty quickly — if he wants to. MLB Network is the obvious name to watch.

And yes, that would be a home run.

item.title: Republicans say they'd welcome Fetterman as GOP eyes bigger Senate majority
item.description: John Thune says Republicans would welcome John Fetterman if he leaves the Democratic Party, as the senator's pro-Israel stance isolates him.
item.content:encoded:
Republicans are ready to embrace a maverick Democrat eying an exit from the party to further grow their thin majority.

Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., recently drew a sharp line in the sand on what it would take to leave the Democratic Party, and Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., would be happy to have him officially cross the aisle.

"Yes, we would welcome him," Thune said. "And I haven't talked to him recently. I have had conversations with him in the past, as have many of my colleagues, about the challenges he faces in his caucus. And, you know, there are many of us who I think would welcome the opportunity to have him join the Republican conference."

TRUMP FACES REPUBLICAN REBELLION OVER DAYLIGHT SAVING TIME AS SENATE BILL NEARS DEAD END

Fetterman, who helped clinch a historically thin Democratic majority in 2022 running on a progressive platform, has transformed into a staunch advocate for Israel at the same time his Democratic colleagues in the Senate have begun pulling away from supporting the Jewish State.

He has routinely broken with his party in his support of the war in Iran and Israel’s campaign against Hamas in Gaza. And that has fueled many of his policy positions on key votes in the upper chamber.

Fetterman told the Hill earlier this month that if Democrats continued to pull further and further away from supporting Israel, he’d leave.

"If our party ever becomes — and just makes it official — the anti-Israel party, that’s when I would leave because that’s been a moral clarity for me," Fetterman said.

WARREN DEFIES SCHUMER AS DEM CIVIL WAR EXPLODES OVER MUST-WIN SENATE RACE

Each time Senate Democrats have brought forward a war powers resolution to rein in Trump’s authority in the region, and force the administration to come to Congress for approval, Fetterman has joined Republicans to vote it down.

Earlier this year, Fetterman argued, "Something like this is much more important than just voting what your base might demand."

"Because I think things are much bigger and more important than that," he continued. "And Iran with a nuclear bomb is one of those things."

DEMS SCORE WIN AS GOP SENATOR HELPS ADVANCE IRAN WAR POWERS RESOLUTION

Fetterman’s edict on his party’s possible evolution came after 103 House Democrats — over half of the caucus — voted to end $3.3 billion in taxpayer-funded aid to Israel.

And earlier this year, Senate Democrats showed their slow creep backward against Israel, too, following the release of photos of starving children in Gaza.

Forty Senate Democrats voted to halt arms sales and military bulldozers to Israel as an act of protest against Trump’s war in Iran in April.

The two resolutions from Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., combined would have blocked nearly $500 million in arms and equipment sales to Israel. One resolution would have halted the sale of roughly $295 million in Caterpillar bulldozers, while the other would have stopped the sale of nearly $152 million worth of 1,000-pound bombs.

Still, Thune said as much as Republicans would want to add him to their ranks, particularly with his seat up in 2028, the decision remains with Fetterman.

"I mean, this is a party that's moving further and further, more radically, and to the more extreme left on a lot of issues," Thune said. "And I think people like John Fetterman, who have views that probably reflect a more moderate or at least historically moderate Democrat position, are really kind of people without a home."

"So if he ever comes to the conclusion that he's ready to move, we would certainly welcome it," he continued.

item.title: ‘Hero' mom dies after using last strength to save 4-year-old son from Florida river
item.description: Nicole Grayson, 43, jumped into the St. Johns River at Ed Stone Park to save her 4-year-old son, using all her strength to keep him above water.
item.content:encoded:
A Florida "hero" mom died after using all her strength to keep her 4-year-old son above water when he fell into the St. Johns River, allowing rescuers to pull the boy to safety, authorities and her family said.

Nicole Grayson, 43, jumped into the river after her young son fell into the water at Ed Stone Park, west of DeLand, around 4 p.m. Saturday, according to the Volusia Sheriff’s Office.

Investigators said the child had been playing with an unidentified object near a wooden fence along the river’s edge when he dropped it over the barrier. The boy fell into the river while attempting to retrieve it.

OFF-DUTY ICE OFFICER RELIES ON 'SIXTH SENSE' TO SAVE DROWNING CHILD BEFORE DELIVERING MESSAGE TO CRITICS

Grayson immediately went in after him.

"My guess is he was trying to get it. He slipped in the water, deep, he slipped away in the water," Grayson’s oldest son, Dafino Devega, told FOX 35. "And then my mom, I guess she panicked, and she went in the water and tried to save him."

CROCODILE ATTACK HORROR UNFOLDS AS COUPLE TRIES TO SAVE VICTIM: ‘WE FELT SO HELPLESS’

As Grayson struggled to keep her son above the surface, a witness at the park flagged down a passing boater, who threw two life preservers toward the mother and child, the sheriff’s office said.

Grayson eventually became submerged while continuing to hold her son above the water.

A frantic 911 caller told dispatchers that rescuers had managed to reach the child but were still struggling to recover his mother.

FIREFIGHTERS, POLICE PULL YOUNG WOMAN FROM SEWAGE-FILLED RAVINE AFTER DAYS TRAPPED IN 104-DEGREE HEAT

"Hey 911, can you come to Ed Stone Park?" the caller said in audio obtained by FOX 35. "We have a mother that’s drowned, and we got the baby, but we can’t get the mama."

SEND US A TIP

The boater maneuvered close enough to pull the 4-year-old aboard. The boy was unharmed, according to authorities.

Witness Jon Reaka said Grayson appeared to expend all her energy ensuring her son survived.

"She spent all her energy trying to get her kid up over the edge," Reaka told FOX 35.

Reaka jumped over a railing and onto the boat, where he helped pull Grayson from the river. She was unresponsive when she was brought aboard.

GET BREAKING NEWS ALERTS

"I jumped over the rail, jumped onto the boat and pulled the lady up onto the boat and, you know, we started CPR — I started CPR and mouth-to-mouth immediately," Reaka said.

Emergency medical personnel took over lifesaving measures after arriving at the park. Grayson was rushed to a hospital, where efforts to revive her continued for several hours.

She died in the intensive care unit around 10 p.m. Saturday, the sheriff’s office said.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

"I tried everything I could do to save her," Reaka told FOX 35. "You know, I feel horrible. I mean, it’s so sad what happened."

Grayson was a mother of six whose final act reflected the fiercely protective way she had lived, her family said.

"That’s the type of mom that she was; she was a hero," Devega said.

"My mom, she was a strong woman," he added. "She would do anything to keep her kids safe."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE U.S. NEWS

FOLLOW US ON X

Volusia County Sheriff Mike Chitwood praised the witnesses and the boater who rushed to help Grayson and her son.

"Sheriff Chitwood and the entire Volusia Sheriff's Office send sincere thanks all who jumped in to help with the rescue, and deepest condolences to Ms. Grayson's family and friends for their loss of a mother who gave her life to save her son," the department said in a statement.

GOT A TIP?

Chitwood said Grayson would be remembered as a hero, "not just because of her final heroic act, but because she was a mom who would do anything for her kids."

item.title: Experts finally solve mystery of teenage Revolutionary War soldier 246 years after his death
item.description: DNA analysis has identified Private John Pumphrey, a teenager from a prominent Maryland family who died at the Battle of Camden in South Carolina in 1780.
item.content:encoded:
The remains of a teenage Revolutionary War soldier have finally been identified — over 246 years after he was buried in an unmarked grave.

The discovery, which researchers believe is the first identification of a Revolutionary War soldier using DNA and forensic genetic genealogy, was announced in June.

Researchers at FHD Forensics used advanced DNA analysis and forensic genetic genealogy to identify the remains as Private John Pumphrey, a teenager who died at the Battle of Camden in South Carolina.

MYSTERIOUS 18TH-CENTURY WARSHIP UNEARTHED AT GROUND ZERO SITE GETS NEW HOME

The Battle of Camden was one of the Continental Army's worst defeats during the Revolutionary War, and hundreds of American soldiers died during the engagement.

Officials believe Pumphrey enlisted in 1777, when he was between 13 and 15 years old — meaning he was between 16 and 18 years of age when he died on Aug. 16, 1780.

Pumphrey's remains were uncovered at the battlefield site in 2022 during a University of South Carolina excavation.

In a June news release from FHD Forensics, officials said a tiny piece of the soldier's skull was used to extract DNA, allowing researchers to uncover his identity before he was reburied with military honors in 2024.

REVOLUTIONARY WAR ARTIFACTS UNEARTHED AT SITE OF INFAMOUS COLONIAL AMERICAN HEIST: 'VERY SURPRISED'

"He would have been one of the youngest members of George Washington's revered 'Old Line,'" the release said.

It was not "highly unusual" for young boys to enlist in the Continental Army, though it was rare, said Allison Peacock, president of FHD Forensics.

Peacock told Fox News Digital that it was a "pretty regular occurrence for young boys to volunteer."

She added, "They were supposed to be at a certain age, but that rule was [broken] all the time, from what we understand."

HIDDEN REVOLUTIONARY WAR BAKE HOUSE IS UNCOVERED AFTER SPENDING CENTURIES UNDERGROUND

Researchers were initially surprised, she said, because they knew from anthropological analysis that the soldier was very young — but they struggled to understand why someone like him had enlisted.

"When we started seeing the families he was related to, they were all prominent Anne Arundel County, Maryland, founding families — the gentry class," she said.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"The Pumphreys were very prominent in lumber, building and land ownership, so none of our historians or the people we were working with thought it made sense for a boy that young to come from one of those families."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

After uncovering details about the turmoil surrounding his father's estate following his parents' deaths and learning that his family lived much closer to Baltimore than they had realized, they said his decision to enlist in Baltimore made much more sense.

"Knowing those little details to put the story together changed everything," Peacock said. 

"When you see the proximity to Baltimore and you see what was going on in his family and his military record, you get a picture of what led this boy to go enlist in Baltimore."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

Peacock said she is confident the remaining unidentified Camden soldiers can also be identified using the same techniques, provided the team can secure funding.

"It is our understanding that it is the first time it's ever happened," she said.

"We're already rewriting the story of the battle," added Peacock. "We're learning things that historians had no way of knowing until we got into the DNA."

item.title: Saquon Barkley tackling streaming maze as NFL keeps growing worldwide: 'I'm all about eyes'
item.description: Saquon Barkley tells Fox News Digital why he partnered with DIRECTV and his vision for growing the NFL globally before facing the Jacksonville Jaguars in London.
item.content:encoded:
Saquon Barkley knows that he is "quote unquote, one of the faces" of the National Football League. It is not an easy title to hold, but being a No. 2 overall pick turned 2,000-yard rusher, Offensive Player of the Year and Super Bowl winner will do that to you.

Barkley's very first game as a Philadelphia Eagle was played in Brazil, and this year, the team will travel to London in Week 5 to take on the Jacksonville Jaguars.

But as the NFL continues to grow its audience and lap the field, Barkley is "all about eyes."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"It’s so important that we are traveling across the country to play the game. The more eyes you can get on it, the better it’s going to be," Barkley told Fox News Digital in a recent interview.

"The more people are going to want to grow up and play football, want to be involved. It’s really kind of crazy that football is probably the biggest sport, and we really haven’t even scratched the surface yet, to be completely honest. There’s so many more territories that we just take over, and I think the NFL is doing a really good job of that.

"I’m a fan of getting as many eyeballs as we can in the game, help growing the game. Anytime I can get an opportunity to play in front of someone that’s never got to watch the game, I want to try to make it memorable and have an impact on them… So right now, I’m all about eyes, eyes, eyes on the game, and let’s grow this game as big as we can, and let’s have an impact on as many people as we can."

That's why Barkley has joined forces with DIRECTV ahead of his ninth NFL season. As part of the campaign, Barkley recorded a spot that turned his iconic hurdle into a humorous metaphor for the obstacles football fans now jump through just to find a game. It shows why Barkley wants diehard fans to keep watching games, dare we say, the old-fashioned way.

GEORGIA RUNNING BACK SUFFERS 'SERIOUS INJURIES' FROM ATV 'ACCIDENT,' COACH KIRBY SMART SAYS

"It’s going to be crazy in 10 to 15 years, the way you’re going to be able to watch football games," Barkley said of today's streaming-service takeover.

"It’s kind of like what I grew up on, and now to be able to have the opportunity to work with them, and to be able to not only bring it in the NFL level, but also in the collegiate level where the game continues to grow and how much eyes are continuing to get on this football game, I think it’s the only way. I think it’s the right way to do it, and for me to be able to have the opportunity to work with DIRECTV, someone I’ve been a fan of for a very long time and be able to shoot a spot with them, it was awesome."

Barkley has accomplished just about everything a football player could dream of. He can retire right now and continue the golf grind (he claims Philly's top five courses trump New Jersey's top five, although Barkley and this writer obliged that Pine Valley is a Philadelphia course). But as to why he's still grinding, his answer is simple.

"I love it," he said.

"It’s something I’m very passionate about. My son SJ, he’s already passionate about football. I was around the same age, two or three, where I said this is something that I want to do, and I’m living my dream. I’m living the dream that I always wanted to live. So, I’m having fun with it, and as long as God keeps me healthy, and they keep giving me contracts and opportunities, as long as they want to keep me in this league, I’ll try to play as long as I can....

"Whether it’s a year, whether it’s five years, there’s going to be a time where I’m like, 'Damn, I wish I could go back into that locker room and just spend one more day with the boys, spend one more day in that weight room, spend one more day running out that tunnel.' It’s going to be a mess. So, I try to enjoy it right now."

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Robot bird swims underwater then flies away
item.description: MIT and EPFL researchers built an 8.8-ounce robotic bird that flies, dives underwater and launches back into the air using only flapping wings.
item.content:encoded:
Watching a diving bird vanish beneath the water can feel almost unreal. One moment it cruises through the sky. Seconds later, it swims after prey before returning to the air. Around 100 bird species can move between those two environments. Engineers have found that natural ability incredibly difficult to recreate at a small scale.

Now, researchers at MIT and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne, known as EPFL, have built a robotic bird that can complete the entire journey. The 8.8-ounce robot flies, plunges into water and swims below the surface. Then it launches back into the air using the same set of flapping wings.

NEW YORK SCHOOL DISTRICT TO DEPLOY HUMANOID ROBOT IN CLASSROOMS THIS FALL

According to the research team, it marks the first bird-scale robot to complete that full cycle through flapping motion alone. The research appeared in the journal Science.

Free live CyberGuy class: Sick of Spam? Join us July 22

Join us TODAY, Wednesday, July 22, at 1 PM ET for a free CyberGuy Live class that will help you cut down on robocalls, spam texts, junk email and other unwanted messages. Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson will walk you step by step through simple ways to filter spam, clean up your inbox and recognize the messages that could put your personal information at risk. No technical experience is needed. You’ll also receive our spam-stopping checklist, and every registrant will get a link to the class recording afterward.

Reserve your free spot today at CyberGuyLive.com.

Most amphibious robots use separate systems for air and water. This mechanical bird takes a simpler approach. The robot relies on flapping wings instead of propellers. It also completes its water launch without legs or a complicated wing-folding mechanism.

That design presents a major engineering problem. Water is about 800 times denser than air, so wings face far greater resistance once they enter the water. To handle that dramatic change, the robot adjusts its flapping speed. Its flexible wings also change shape as the surrounding pressure increases.

In the air, the robot can flap its wings up to 11 times per second. Underwater, its flapping rate ranges from 0.1 to 6 times per second. Meanwhile, water pressure can bend the wings by as much as 90%. That flexibility shortens the effective sweep of each stroke and reduces the load on the motor.

The same wings become more effective for flight once the robot reaches the air. Researchers also made the machine neutrally buoyant. As a result, it neither rises nor sinks on its own while underwater. That balance helps conserve battery power because the robot spends less energy fighting buoyancy.

The hardest part begins when the robot tries to leave the water. It completes the transition in under one second using about eight to 10 wingbeats. However, the maneuver requires a careful combination of wing flexibility, tail placement and launch angle.

Researchers found that moderately flexible wings worked best. A rigid wing struggles to adapt underwater, while excessive flexibility reduces the force needed for takeoff. The tail also needs to remain short and close to the body. Otherwise, it can drag through the water and pull the robot back down. An exit angle near 70 degrees produced the strongest results. A flatter approach creates too much tail drag. A nearly vertical launch can make the robot tip backward into the water.

ZELENSKYY ANNOUNCES 'THE FUTURE IS HERE' AFTER WAR'S FIRST ALL-ROBOT CAPTURE

The machine also gives scientists a new way to study real diving birds. Tracking the movement of a live bird beneath the surface can be difficult. With a robot, researchers can adjust one feature and measure how that change affects performance.

For example, many diving birds reduce their wingspan while swimming. Researchers have often connected that behavior with lower energy use. The robot's results suggest that shorter underwater strokes may instead help birds increase speed.

The team also compared the machine's propulsion efficiency with that of real birds. Both fell within a Strouhal number range of 0.2 to 0.4, which researchers associate with efficient movement. However, body size can change the launch strategy. Heavier diving birds may use their legs to help push away from the water, while this lightweight robot relies entirely on its wings.

The robot's most efficient travel mode depends on the distance ahead. According to the team's data, flying uses less energy once a journey extends beyond roughly 51 feet. For shorter trips, staying underwater may make more sense.

Water creates heavy resistance, so swimming becomes increasingly costly over longer distances. A future robot could use that difference to plan its route. It might swim toward a nearby target, surface and then fly to a more distant location.

The prototype costs around $300 in materials and uses parts that researchers can source commercially. The team also released open CAD files for the project. That could allow universities and other builders with access to a 3D printer to reproduce the design.

Eventually, the robotic bird could help scientists monitor waterways and coastal environments. Researchers imagine launching it from shore or a boat. It could fly toward an area of interest, dive to collect a sample and then return with the data.

Potential missions could include taking measurements near an iceberg or observing marine wildlife from a safer distance. Flapping wings may also offer practical advantages in those environments. They avoid exposed high-speed propeller blades and could produce less noise around animals. Underwater, flexible wings may tolerate contact with debris better than rigid propellers.

INSIDE CHINA’S AI ‘WOLF PACK’ DRONES BUILT WITH TAIWAN CONFLICT IN MIND

The current prototype still relies on human control during key parts of its journey. Researchers manually launched the robot during several tests. Timers or basic triggers also activated parts of its diving and swimming behavior.

Autonomous navigation represents the next major step. The robot would need to recognize its surroundings and control each transition without human assistance. Salt water creates another hurdle. Testing has focused on fresh water, so future versions will need stronger protection against corrosion. Longer range and improved endurance would also make the robot more useful outside controlled experiments.

You probably will not see robotic birds patrolling your local beach anytime soon. Still, this project shows how future drones could reach places that traditional flying machines struggle to explore. A single robot could gather information above the water before taking measurements below the surface. That flexibility could reduce the need for separate aircraft and underwater vehicles.

The low material cost also matters. Smaller research teams could experiment with the design without investing in an expensive custom platform. As navigation and battery performance improve, machines like this could make environmental monitoring more accessible.

What stands out here is how much work the researchers get from one flexible set of wings. The robot changes its flapping speed, while the wings naturally bend to handle the resistance underwater. The launch back into the air makes this more than another bird-inspired drone. Pulling off that transition requires the right wing stiffness and a carefully controlled exit angle. The prototype still needs autonomous controls and better protection for saltwater. Even so, its $300 material cost and open design could give other researchers an affordable foundation to build on.

Would robotic birds collecting environmental data make you feel hopeful about conservation, or uneasy about machines blending into natural habitats? Let us know by writing to us at Cyberguy.com.

Sign up for my FREE CyberGuy Report

Copyright 2026 CyberGuy.com. All rights reserved.

item.title: Dem Katie Porter makes surprising announcement about political future
item.description: Katie Porter says she is closing the door on being a candidate after finishing fifth in the California governor race amid staff abuse allegations.
item.content:encoded:
Former Rep. Katie Porter, D-Calif., appears to be retiring from politics, telling the Sacramento Bee that it's unlikely she runs for office again after a series of failed bids for national positions.

Porter, fresh off a fifth-place finish in the California governor's race, told the Bee "I am closing the door on being a candidate," in a Friday interview with the outlet.

"I cannot foresee that path back," she added.

Porter, who left her Congressional seat to run for the Senate seat vacated by the late Dianne Feinstein in 2023, also lost that race, garnering over 1 million votes, but still falling short of the top two candidates, Steve Garvey and eventual winner Sen. Adam Schiff.

KATIE PORTER’S ATTEMPT TO BRUSH OFF VIRAL OUTBURST BACKFIRES AS CRITICS TORCH ‘WORST POLITICAL AD’

Both campaigns for the high-profile national offices were marred by personal scandals and public backlash to reports of Porter's private behavior.

In 2023, while running for Senate, Porter responded to claims that she abused members of her staff, including during one alleged incident in which she chastised a staffer for catching COVID-19.

The texts between Porter and her staff member showed Porter saying "you gave me COVID" and that Porter's "children have nobody to care for them."

Porter denied firing the staffer for the incident and said, "I regret if this employee feels disgruntled."

KATIE PORTER SAYS SHE REGRETS VIRAL OUTBURSTS AT REPORTER, STAFFER

Allegations of toxic behavior were renewed when a leaked video revealed Porter screaming and cursing at another staffer during her gubernatorial campaign in 2025.

"GET OUT OF MY F---ING SHOT" Porter screamed in the now-viral clip.

Porter has also come under fire for the bizarre way she abruptly ended a video interview with a local reporter.

The mounting scandals appeared to affect her electability.

Finishing a respectable third behind Garvey and Schiff in the 2024 non-partisan "jungle primary" for the state's Senate seat, Porter garnered 1.27 million votes from Californians, good for 17.2% of the electorate, according to California's secretary of state.

But in 2025, Porter finished fifth, receiving only 403,908 votes, representing only 4.4% of the electorate, according to the California secretary of state's certified election results.

She told the Sacramento Bee she would be focusing on making social media posts about books going forward.

Fox News Digital contacted Porter for comment.

item.title: Tom Brady's potential WWE storyline with Logan Paul receives support from fellow NFL legend
item.description: Drew Brees says Tom Brady and Logan Paul should schedule a WWE match as their feud escalates from Fanatics Fest to the FIFA World Cup final.
item.content:encoded:
Tom Brady’s potential involvement in a WWE storyline with Logan Paul has been scrutinized over the last few days but the NFL legend finally found some support in a former colleague.

Drew Brees was asked about the idea of Brady and Paul meeting in the ring and appeared to support the idea.

COMPLETE PRO WRESTLING COVERAGE ON FOX NEWS DIGITAL

"They should. They should schedule it," Brees told TMZ Sports.

Brady and Paul have been in a feud since the seven-time Super Bowl dismissed Paul’s athleticism in the ring. The two met at the Fanatics Flag Football Classic, and it appeared, for the time being, that the two put their differences aside.

However, at Fanatics Fest in New York, Brady and Paul met face-to-face. Brady attempted to slap Paul on stage following a larger talk. The two were also seen giving the middle finger to each other while watching the FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

TMZ Sports first reported that Brady was in talks with WWE about a potential appearance in the ring. Fox News Digital later confirmed the report.

It’s unclear whether it would be an actual match or some kind of valet role.

Regardless, Jake Paul had a message for the former New England Patriots and Tampa Bay Buccaneers quarterback.

"I’m honestly p---ed. I wish I was there to back him up," he told Fox News Digital. "But I have one message for Tom. The Paul brothers are coming for you and revenge is sweet. Tom, keep your head on a swivel."

Logan Paul has been sidelined with a triceps injury and his timetable for in-ring action is undetermined at this time.

WWE is set to hold WrestleMania 43 in Saudi Arabia next spring. It will be the first time WrestleMania is held outside the U.S.

item.title: DAVID MARCUS: In booming Detroit, voters skeptical of socialism snake oil
item.description: Detroit voters express doubts about Abdul El-Sayed's far-left Senate bid as the city's revitalization undercuts the socialist message in Michigan.
item.content:encoded:
It is rare to find a bartender who knows how to make a good Vuex Carre, but Emily, at Le Supreme Brasserie, knocked it out of the park. She grew up in the suburbs, but loves living her 20s downtown, and the revitalization of the Motor City she enjoys may be a problem for far-left senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed.

A bit later, on Sunday night, I met Nick, 29, who grew up in Detroit and now works in advertising in Chicago. He marvels at the changes he has seen in his hometown.

"It’s so different now, a bunch of these places didn’t even exist a few years ago," he said.

DEMOCRATS' CIVIL WAR HEADS TO MICHIGAN WHERE PROGRESSIVES FACE BIGGEST TEST YET IN HIGH-STAKES SENATE SHOWDOWN

I heard it over and over again, and I could see it, as well. Along Washington and Woodward, the shops were buzzing, and even the heavy smoke from Canada couldn’t keep people from strolling the thoroughfares and valleys in the grand, sturdy Midwestern brick castle of downtown.

Not far away, earlier in the day, I had met Sean, who was walking his attractive dog and who moved to Detroit in 2019 from Philly. He lives in one of the fancier downtown buildings and loves his adopted hometown, and the lifestyle in the city.

"It’s easy going," he told me. "There is a lot to do. It’s great."

SWING-STATE POLL SPELLS TROUBLE FOR FAR LEFT AS SCHUMER-BACKED CANDIDATE DOMINATES WITH CRITICAL VOTING BLOCS

Sean is a committed Democrat who hates President Donald Trump.

"All that matters is that we elect a Democrat," he said. "R or D, that’s all it comes down to, which is a shame, but it's how things are."

For Sean, the top priority in the Aug. 4 Democratic Senate primary between Abdul El-Sayed and Rep. Haley Stevens is electability. That is not great news for El-Sayed, who has been pushing back against the idea that he is too radical to get elected in purple Michigan.

DAVID MARCUS: SOCIALISM AND EL-SAYED RUN INTO HARSH REALITY IN BLUE-COLLAR MICHIGAN

He has reason to be concerned, as I learned from James, a retired plumber in his 60s, who is Black and has been involved in civil rights movements.

"This just doesn’t feel like that movement I’ve been a part of, it feels different," James told me. "It feels foreign to me."

And he wasn’t alone, as it turns out. The day before, I had met Kelly, an American woman of Iranian descent who warned that today’s far-left candidates like El-Sayed "are just like in Iran." She said Muslim immigrants in nearby Dearborn, are not assimilating.

"When Joe [Biden] opened that border, they were planted there," she said.

Taken together, these recent encounters, from some very different people around Detroit, painted a picture that seems to be increasingly favoring Stevens, which is presumably why Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez did a three-stop tour for El-Sayed.

On Monday, El-Sayid got another boost of sorts as Sen. Elizabeth Warren endorsed him, although frankly, that is about as surprising as Tony the Tiger endorsing Frosted Flakes.

What is missing for El-Sayed – and all of his socialist out-of-town pals – in this city that is booming in comparison to many others in America, is any real, pressing need for socialism. Unlike New York City, where the rent is always "too damn high," in Detroit, affordability is not proving to be the same magical incantation that it was for Gotham Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

Many Democratic voters I talked to wanted a steadier hand on the system, and they want to beat Trump and the GOP. But they don’t want to blow up the system which has been thriving in many ways.

There may be one more trump card to be played in this tight race in the form of Gov. Gretchen Whitmer, who has thus far declined to endorse either El-Sayed or Stevens. As confusing as it may be to Republicans around the country, Whitmer is very popular among Michigan Democrats, in part because of Detroit’s revitalization under her watch.

The stakes of this race for the national Democratic Party could not be higher. It is a true and fair test of whether the far-left wing of the party, led by college-educated Whites and immigrants, can replace the old party of Jefferson and Jackson led by the working class and native minorities.

Another thing that stood out in Motown was that, aside from the Bernie rally itself, not one voter I talked to brought up Israel. One way or the other, this matters because, while El-Sayed’s constant negative focus on Israel may put him on the right side of the issue with most Democrats, these days, it's not a driving issue.

Waiting in the background for the dust to settle is Republican Mike Rogers, who is set to take on the Democratic winner. From his point of his chances.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM DAVID MARCUS

item.title: CNN's Jake Tapper hits Dems for rejecting illegal voting concerns amid New Jersey voter roll controversy
item.description: Jake Tapper says the New Jersey noncitizen voting revelation is "not good" for Democrats who long dismissed concerns about illegal immigrants voting.
item.content:encoded:
CNN anchor Jake Tapper took a swipe at Democrats who have long rejected concerns about illegal immigrants voting in elections following Tuesday's stunning revelation out of New Jersey.

Democratic New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced that thousands of noncitizens had been mistakenly added to voter rolls.

Sherrill said that "roughly 6,600 people" who were never eligible to vote were registered due to the "serious software error" by the state's DMV between June 2023 and June 2024 during the previous administration.

NEW JERSEY REMOVES 6,600 PEOPLE FROM VOTER ROLLS WHO WERE ELIGIBLE TO CAST A BALLOT FOR 2024 ELECTION

She also revealed that "fewer than 400 individuals" among the noncitizens ended up voting in elections.

While covering the breaking news, Tapper acknowledged that the recent development does not benefit Democrats.

"This is not good for Democrats' argument that there's no such thing as undocumented immigrants voting," Tapper said during a panel discussion.

JOHNSON'S STRATEGY TO BYPASS DEMOCRATS' SAVE ACT BLOCKADE SURVIVES KEY VOTE, HANDING GOP BIG WIN

Conservative critics on social media took aim at liberals, suggesting that the New Jersey revelations validate their concerns about noncitizens being able to cast ballots in elections.

Members of the Trump administration have highlighted New Jersey's controversy to push the passing of the SAVE America Act meant to address election security.

Days before Sherrill's disclosure, President Donald Trump gave a primetime address from the White House renewing his call for Congress to pass the SAVE America Act while shedding light on election vulnerabilities in past election cycles and China's effort to meddle in U.S. elections.

CLICK HERE FOR THE LATEST MEDIA AND CULTURE NEWS
