item.title: Growing trend of hating men backfires as anti-marriage culture leaves single women losing out
item.description: With U.S. marriage rates falling and hookup culture alienating young women, a columnist warns that the anti-marriage movement has real consequences.
item.content:encoded:
A growing anti-marriage movement is being fueled by viral TikTok trends and high-profile celebrities painting men as the enemy, but according to a new column, women may ultimately be the ones losing out.

In a USA Today op-ed, columnist Ingrid Jacques warned of a rising wave of "heteropessimism," defining it as women’s growing feelings of disappointment and antagonism toward relationships with men. She argued that viral trends like "divorce glow-ups" and anti-male literature have actively widened the divide between the sexes.

"Studies show that married women are not only happier but also live longer and earn more than their single counterparts," Jacques wrote on Tuesday.

RELATIONSHIP EXPERT CHALLENGES ONE OF AMERICA'S BIGGEST ASSUMPTIONS ABOUT DIVORCE

She explained that the media modern women consume is increasingly hostile toward traditional relationships, pointing to bestselling books like "Yesteryear," which centers on a "tradwife" influencer.

In that novel, Jacques notes, men are reduced to lazy or toxic tropes: "Either a man is an idiotic man-baby who lacks ambition and a brain, or he's a quintessential patriarch who pals around with other sexist pigs in the manosphere. Husbands are at one point referred to as ‘freaks.’"

On Amazon, the book’s description includes, "A gripping, electrifying novel that is as darkly funny as it is frightening, ‘Yesteryear’ is a gimlet-eyed look at tradition, fame, faith, and the grand performance of womanhood."

TRANS ACTOR ELLIOT PAGE ARGUES 'HEALTHY MASCULINITY' IS REJECTING EXPECTATION TO 'SHUT DOWN'

The trend coincides with falling U.S. marriage and birth rates. Official U.S. Census Bureau data from December shows that just 47% of American households in 2025 were married couples, down from nearly 66% 50 years ago. Of those married households, only 37% included their own children under age 18, compared to 54% in 1975.

Jacques linked the shift directly to liberal ideology, noting that left-leaning Americans choose to opt out of traditional family lifestyles "far more often than conservatives."

"Wherever you look these days, women who ‘free’ themselves from the chains of marriage are being celebrated," Jacques wrote. She highlighted cultural figures who publicly champion singlehood or life after divorce, including "Eat, Pray, Love" author Elizabeth Gilbert and model Emily Ratajkowski.

In April, comedian Leslie Jones criticized marriage, saying that most people she knew who got married are now divorced and arguing that "marriage is legalized slavery."

VIRAL NIKKI GLASER CLIP PROMPTS DISCUSSION OVER WHETHER MONOGAMY IS DEAD

A 2024 American Family Survey found that 37% of conservative women and 28% of moderate women, between the ages of 18 and 40, reported being "completely satisfied" with their lives. However, liberal women of the same age reported only 12% feeling the same way.

The sentiment has also taken over social media through trends like the TikTok "divorce glow up," where women post before-and-after photos celebrating their split. Jacques noted that in these videos, the women "typically look happier and more radiant in the ‘after’ photo."

Citing Louise Perry, author of "The Case Against the Sexual Revolution," Jacques argued that young women are being alienated after getting "burned by hookup culture" and lacking real friendships with the opposite sex.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

Jacques argued that, like Perry, returning to marriage, not abandoning men altogether, is the remedy for the cultural divide.

"That won’t be true for everyone, of course," Jacques concluded. "But men and women need to find a way back to each other. Discounting marriage won’t get them there."

item.title: When New York City comes second: The problem with an Israel-first mayor
item.description: New York City elected a mayor to fix housing, crime and schools, but Zohran Mamdani's defining obsession appears to be Israel, not the five boroughs.
item.content:encoded:
For a man elected to run New York City, Zohran Mamdani spends a remarkable amount of time thinking about Israel.

Before he has solved the city’s affordability crisis, before New Yorkers feel safer on the subway, before families see cleaner streets, better schools or a government that simply works, the socialist Mamdani has chosen to make the Jewish state and its leader a defining obsession — Israel and Benjamin Netanyahu.

He has publicly declared that he would seek to have Israel’s prime minister arrested if Netanyahu came to New York. Whether or not such a thing could ever happen is beside the point. A New York City mayor has no meaningful role in international criminal law or American foreign policy. Yet this became one of Mamdani’s earliest and most high-profile priorities, and now he admits he doesn't have the legal authority to do so.

MAMDANI SAYS NYC IS EXPLORING WHETHER IT CAN ARREST NETANYAHU IF ISRAELI LEADER VISITS FOR UN GENERAL ASSEMBLY

Think about that. The mayor of America’s largest city looked across the ocean before looking across his own boroughs. That tells you something. It tells you where his passion lives. It tells you what keeps him up at night, and it tells you that, for all the campaign rhetoric about housing, transit and working families, Israel occupies an extraordinary place in his political imagination.

So, I’m calling him the first MIGA mayor: Make Israel Great Again. The pattern did not begin there.

Among the earliest actions of his new administration was revoking New York City’s adoption of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance’s working definition of antisemitism. Supporters argued that the definition could chill legitimate criticism of Israel. Critics saw something very different: a city stepping away from one of the world’s most widely used tools for recognizing modern antisemitism at a time when antisemitic incidents continue to rise.

That decision sent a message. Whether intended or not, many Jewish New Yorkers heard it loud and clear.

The administration also rolled back an executive order adopted under Mayor Eric Adams that had sought to prevent city agencies from discriminating against Israel, Israeli citizens and Israel-linked businesses through municipal contracting or procurement.

Again, supporters defended the change. Again, many Jewish New Yorkers saw a familiar pattern. One decision can be explained away. Two decisions become a trend. Three decisions become an agenda.

Then came reports that the administration’s international affairs office had planned a meeting with Iran’s ambassador to the United Nations before the proposal was abandoned. Mamdani later said he had not approved the meeting and called it a mistake. Perhaps. But the contrast was impossible to ignore.

THE US-ISRAEL STRATEGIC ALLIANCE – PARTNERSHIP, NOT CHARITY

An administration willing to explore dialogue with representatives of the Iranian regime while publicly entertaining the arrest of the elected leader of Israel naturally raises questions about its priorities.

None of this exists in isolation. Mamdani proudly identifies with a political movement in which hostility toward Israel has become increasingly central. For years, we have watched the same progression.

Occupy Wall Street, campus BDS campaigns, academic boycotts, the Democratic Socialists of America, the "Squad." Every movement arrives promising economic justice.

Every movement eventually seems to discover that Israel has somehow become one of its defining moral causes. No country receives comparable scrutiny. No democracy is held to quite the same standard. No conflict consumes so much attention.

I know these worlds well. While I now live in Florida, I grew up in Brooklyn Democratic politics, where endorsements mattered, relationships mattered and neighborhood concerns came first.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

Later, my professional life became devoted to fighting antisemitism and introducing Americans to the Israel that headlines rarely capture: its innovation, diversity, culture, resilience and humanity. For decades those two worlds barely intersected. Then New York elected Zohran Mamdani. Now they have collided.

Many people were stunned to hear that Jewish voters helped elect him. The reality is more nuanced.

Available exit polling suggests that most Jewish voters supported Andrew Cuomo, not Mamdani. Yet a meaningful minority, particularly younger and more progressive Jews, backed him. Every voter has his or her reasons. Some cared almost exclusively about affordability, some believed municipal politics and Middle East politics should remain separate, and some assumed campaign rhetoric would give way to pragmatic governing.

The first months of this administration suggest otherwise. Israel is not an occasional topic for this mayor. It is a recurring theme. And that matters because New York is home to the largest Jewish community outside Israel.

For millions of New Yorkers, Israel is not an abstract foreign policy debate.

It is family.

It is faith.

It is history.

It is identity.

SIGN UP FOR ANTISEMITISM EXPOSED NEWSLETTER

When elected officials repeatedly single out the world’s only Jewish state for exceptional condemnation, many Jews do not experience that as ordinary political disagreement. It’s antisemitism.

They experience it as part of a climate that has made Jewish schools hire more security, synagogues install more barriers and Jewish students wonder whether expressing a connection to Israel will make them targets.

New Yorkers elected a mayor to obsess over New York, over crime, over housing, over sanitation, over schools. Over whether a family can afford to stay in the city.

Instead, they have a mayor who appears to reserve his greatest passion for litigating the legitimacy of a country nearly six thousand miles away. Every mayor leaves a signature. Some build parks, some reduce crime and some transform neighborhoods.

Zohran Mamdani is writing a different legacy. He is becoming the mayor who cannot stop talking about Israel while New Yorkers wait for him to focus on New York.

The Big Apple deserves a mayor whose first priority is the people who elected him. Instead, it has one who seems determined to fight battles far beyond the five boroughs.

item.title: Michelin-star chef reveals what's replacing hospital food Americans love to hate
item.description: Chef Geoffrey Zakarian reveals how he overhauled Tampa General Hospital's menu on a budget while eliminating seed oils and sugar and improving patient health.
item.content:encoded:
"Hospital food" has long been synonymous with packaged, ultraprocessed meals — despite the fact that hospitals are meant to promote healing.

In an exclusive interview with Fox News Digital at the Great American State Fair in Washington, D.C., Michelin-star chef Geoffrey Zakarian shared his initiative to improve what is served in U.S. hospitals, following the concept that "food is medicine."

For the last three years, Zakarian said he’s been working on changing hospital food — most notably at Tampa General, which is a $4 billion nonprofit, 1,000-bed public hospital.

RFK HAILS FIRST US HOSPITAL TO PROMISE HEALTHIER MEALS: 'WE HAVE A TEMPLATE'

On Thursday, July 16, Tampa General Hospital CEO John Couris signed the "Make Hospital Food Healthier" pledge during a news conference attended by Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Secretary Brooke Rollins and Zakarian.

The pledge commits the hospital to serving more nutritious, minimally processed meals to patients, building on Zakarian's menu overhaul.

"We’ve changed the entire menu," the celebrity chef told Fox News Digital. "We've taken all seed oils out, sugar, maltodextrin. Anything bad in a box is gone. Anything good is brought in."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE HEALTH STORIES

"We're serving mac and cheese. We're serving hanger steak and fries. We're serving chicken with couscous. We do all our protein shakes. No protein shakes in the box anymore. We do fruit shakes, smoothies, fresh sorbet," the chef listed. "So, you're eating like your grandmother's cooking for you."

CMS Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz has also supported Zakarian in this mission, telling Fox News Digital that the chef has "revolutionized our thinking" around hospital food.

"How do we get the hospitals to do what [Zakarian] has been doing the frontier work on, get them to actually embrace food as part of the healing process?" he questioned. "Because most patients, and most doctors, know that the food in the hospital is not going to make you better."

Oz noted that while hospital food may provide some nutrients, it is generally not seen as contributing meaningfully to patients' recovery.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

The biggest challenge to getting better food into hospital systems has been cost, Zakarian pointed out.

While "everything is fractionalized," what’s left over after paying for supplies is put toward food, which generally "ain’t much," he said.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

"It's, like, $3.50 a person per plate, which is an extraordinarily small amount," the chef said. "You go to McDonald's, and you spend 12 bucks for a happy meal, and we give you $3.50 in a hospital. So that's an unhappy meal as far as I'm concerned."

Instead of charging hospitals for each plate served, Zakarian's approach relies on a flat annual fee, saving money through a smaller, higher-quality menu.

"Yummy food, not just food to fill people up," he said. "Nutritious food, bioavailable food, food that's satiating."

"I'm cooking like I would cook in a restaurant. I'm cooking like my mother cooked for me. There's no difference."

The chef continued, "When you're lying in bed in a hospital gown, you feel alone, you feel exposed, you feel sick … When you have a fresh protein shake or a fresh healthy smoothie, or you can order steak frites ... that [makes] your day."

Freelance writer Teresa Mull contributed to this report.

item.title: New FDA moves are boosting American jobs and protecting our medicine supply
item.description: The FDA PreCheck pilot program aims to slash delays at new domestic pharmaceutical facilities, with seven major companies already breaking ground.
item.content:encoded:
For decades, U.S. policymakers have highlighted the vulnerabilities of relying on foreign supply chains for essential medicines. Yet over 70% of the primary manufacturing facilities for prescriptions filled in the United States remain outside our borders. Over time, a suffocating web of state and federal regulations meant that companies investing millions or billions to build facilities in the U.S. would see those investments sit idle for up to 14 months waiting for standard post-construction reviews. This paralysis has driven supply chains further overseas, hurting the Americans who rely on life-saving cures and treatments.

When critical supply chains are anchored half a world away, American patients are left exposed to dangerous shortages and American workers are stripped of high-paying employment opportunities.

President Donald Trump prioritized solving this problem by signing Executive Order 14293, "Regulatory Relief to Promote Domestic Production of Critical Medicines," directing the FDA to cut red tape, incentivize domestic manufacturing and secure medicines for American patients. The FDA took that directive urgently. Moving into mid-July, we are aggressively executing on multiple fronts to spark a domestic manufacturing renaissance – removing regulatory roadblocks and creating an environment where American production can outcompete the rest of the world.

TRUMP’S DRUG PRICE WAR WITH EUROPE AIMS TO MAKE THINGS CHEAPER FOR AMERICANS

To rev up our domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing base from the ground up, the FDA recently launched the PreCheck Pilot Program. Designed to clear the hurdles that stall new manufacturing facility builds in the U.S., this program is already unleashing a historic wave of domestic job creation, proving we can secure our health while building a powerhouse economy.

The program operates in two distinct phases. In the early Facility Readiness Phase, manufacturers receive technical and regulatory feedback before operations begin, preventing costly compliance mistakes. Next, the Application Submission Phase features enhanced FDA engagement to quickly resolve manufacturing questions, expedite the review of facility information and fast-track inspections.

By identifying potential issues early, the PreCheck program reduces delays, improves regulatory predictability and accelerates the availability of U.S.-manufactured medicines. This ensures next-generation medical breakthroughs are researched, developed and manufactured right here at home by leveraging our greatest resource – the extraordinary talent, grit and might of the American workforce.

Following our selection of the program's first seven corporate participants – including Eli Lilly, Regeneron, Amneal, Cellares, Fujifilm Biotechnologies, Kriya Therapeutics and Kyowa Kirin – the FDA is actively working alongside these innovators as they break ground and scale operations this summer. These companies are building state-of-the-art facilities to manufacture everything from innovative cell and gene therapies to everyday IV bags.

Crucially, these first seven participants alone will anchor thousands of long-term, high-wage careers for American scientists, engineers, technicians and operators, while instantly fueling thousands of jobs across the heartland.

These are the kinds of stable, family-sustaining careers that anchor communities for generations, helping revitalize local economies and ensuring our brightest minds and hardest workers can build prosperous careers right in their home neighborhoods.

TRUMP EXPANDS TRUMPRX PRESCRIPTION DRUG DISCOUNT PROGRAM TO MORE THAN 800 MEDICATIONS

In addition to this pilot, the FDA is moving forward with newly released draft regulations to streamline pathways for distributed manufacturing and provide greater transparency regarding foreign medicine suppliers. We are also reimagining the incentives within our control.

While the FDA is required to prioritize applications based on urgent public health needs, we are now also prioritizing applications for older, generic medicines that utilize domestic supply chains. These lower-cost medicines make up roughly 90% of all prescriptions written in the U.S., yet they are the very drugs most frequently manufactured overseas. Moving production back home means bringing thousands more stable manufacturing jobs to American factories.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

When a parent goes to the pharmacy for a child’s antibiotic or a patient fights cancer, they should not have to worry about whether a factory closure overseas will cut off their lifeline. Onshoring pharmaceuticals will require a synchronized effort across HHS, private industry and state and local communities, and it is integral to the health, national security and economic prosperity of the American people.

Securing our factories is only one piece of the puzzle; we must also secure the science that feeds them. Under the leadership of President Donald Trump and Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the Department of Health and Human Services recently launched "Operation TrialBlazer," a historic initiative designed to bring clinical trials back to American soil by modernizing drug development and slashing regulatory burdens. Making the United States the most attractive place to conduct clinical research ensures that laboratory discoveries translate directly into high-tech production jobs on American soil.

Through the PreCheck program, aggressive regulatory reform and domestic prioritization, the FDA is acting as a collaborative catalyst for domestic growth. We are protecting patients, fueling a high-tech economic renaissance and restoring American independence by putting American workers first.

item.title: New report uncovers education groups' DEI push to shape how artificial intelligence is used in classrooms
item.description: A report alleges top teachers unions are embedding DEI principles into AI guidance for classrooms through equity and algorithmic bias frameworks.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: The nation's top teacher unions and education groups are allegedly embedding diversity, equity and inclusion principles into AI guidance for classrooms as schools increasingly adopt the technology, according to a sweeping report.

The report by Defending Education, an education watchdog, examines AI guidance, model policies and training materials from the National Education Association (NEA), American Federation of Teachers (AFT) and other education groups, arguing they promote AI policies in the classroom centered on "equity" and "algorithmic bias."

"Teachers unions and associations like the NEA and AFT at this point are nothing more than left-wing political operations funneling millions of dollars into promoting divisive DEI and gender ideologies in classrooms," Defending Education investigative reporter Casey Ryan told Fox News Digital.

Ryan said the education groups are seeking "to use AI as their next frontier to reach school children in an effort to promote their worldview onto America's next generation," adding that they want to shape AI before it becomes widely used in classrooms.

FAR-LEFT ACTIVIST CONFERENCE AT TEACHERS UNION HQ RAISES CLASSROOM CONCERNS

"We cannot allow that to happen," he said. "It is imperative that concerned parents and families speak up now to ensure that the integration of AI into our schools benefits all students, rather than serve as another tool to enforce a problematic agenda."

According to the report, "algorithmic bias" is the belief that AI reflects the views of its creators, often "White or Asian men," prompting education groups to promote "equity" in AI used in classrooms to "ensure that students receive politicized answers and receive a politicized education."

"The integration of AI into education is not a matter of if but when," the report said.

Researchers found the American Federation of Teachers (AFT), which represents more than 1.8 million members, launched the National Academy for AI Instruction, a $23 million initiative with Microsoft, OpenAI and Anthropic that the report said is designed to establish AI "guardrails" for educators.

AI CHATBOTS TAKE HEAT OVER LEFT-WING BIAS: ‘NO LONGER BE CONSIDERED NEUTRAL’

"These are three of the largest corporations building AI that are used on a global scale and are the AI programs already used by many school districts," the report said.

According to the report, a 2025 AFT resolution warned that AI "has the potential to help personalize instruction, reduce administrative burden and make learning more engaging, but without proper guardrails it can perpetuate bias, invade privacy, and undermine educational equity, job security and the professional judgment of educators."

"This program is imbued with the idea from AFT’s resolution that ‘bias’ and ‘equity’ are issues within AI in schools," the report said.

Researchers examined the NEA's AI in Education Hub, which the union says is "grounded in equity, ethics, and educator voice," along with a model policy requiring AI vendors to meet standards for "transparency, equity, and ethical decision-making" and undergo audits for "algorithmic bias."

STUDY FINDS MORE AI PRAISE FOR BLACK STUDENTS, SOFTER TREATMENT OF FEMALES

The report highlights the California Teachers Association’s (CTA) role in developing state AI guidance for K-12 schools that recommends educators "approach AI with an equity lens" while addressing concerns about "equity, bias, and access," and describes AI education as a way to address "systemic bias."

The report also cites articles published by the American Association of School Administrators discussing AI's ties to "White males" and the "New Jim Code."

The National Association of Elementary School Principals (NAESP) published an article encouraging schools to ask AI vendors how they address "algorithmic biases," while the National Association of Secondary School Principals (NASSP) said AI tools should "advance and do not undermine equity," according to the report.

"The goal of these organizations is to embed DEI into AI tools to ensure that students receive politicized answers and receive a politicized education in the nation’s classrooms," the report said.

Fox News Digital reached out to the AFT, NEA, CTA, AASA, NAESP and NASSP for comment.

item.title: TNA star Elayna Black shares why she joined company, how she wanted to be in The Beautiful People
item.description: Elayna Black reveals she emailed TNA at age 12 to join The Beautiful People and grew up idolizing Angelina Love, Velvet Sky and Madison Rayne.
item.content:encoded:
Elayna Black was one of the hottest free agents on the pro wrestling market when she departed WWE in 2025.

Formerly known as Cora Jade in NXT, Black hit the independent circuit performing in places like Atomic Legacy Wrestling, Black Label Pro and Game Changer Wrestling before taking time off. Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) was an option for Black, and she was very familiar with the company’s programming.

She told Fox News Digital she grew up watching TNA and became a fan of Jeff Hardy and the female faction known as The Beautiful People.

COMPLETE PRO WRESTLING COVERAGE ON FOX NEWS DIGITAL

"At the time I started watching TNA, Jeff Hardy was in the company," Black said. "So, I was a huge Jeff Hardy fan … I started watching WWE first. At first, that was the only wrestling company I knew. When you're a kid, you don't really know anything else. And then slowly I discovered TNA and you discover, oh, Jeff Hardy was in WWE, and I was young, so I didn't know. So, just discovering Jeff Hardy was crazy because I was like, I love wearing black nail polish and like I was getting into my little emo phase at the time. So, I loved Jeff Hardy.

"But my all-time favorites were The Beautiful People, specifically when it was the three of them at the time. It was Angelina Love, Velvet Sky, and Madison Rayne. They were like my gods. You could not tell me anything. I was going to be The Beautiful People. I was going to be in The Beautiful People. I remember my first ever phone, like flip phone, this little pink phone when I was in like fifth grade that my parents got me, The Beautiful People's theme song was my first ever ringtone. I emailed TNA when I was like 12 years old trying to convince them to let me be the first ever youngest member of the Beautiful People. Obviously, they never responded, but here I am now. And maybe one day they can walk me to the ring or something. We'll see. But The Beautiful People, those are my girls."

Black finally became a main roster talent for TNA in January.

She told Fox News Digital she didn’t necessarily want to sign anywhere and was trying to do what was best for her.

"I think for a while I didn't really want to sign anywhere," she said. "Obviously, I knew eventually I wanted to be somewhere just because that's what I love to do," she said. "But I think for a while, my mindset was just wanting to prove myself otherwise. And I was having fun on the indies for a little bit, but life happens, some personal things came up, and I felt like I really needed to take a break just for my mental health. I started wrestling training when I was 15. I dropped out of high school to do it.

"The only time I ever had time off was with an injury. So, it's been my life forever. And growing up in wrestling is also kind of difficult. So just taking the time for the first time really and I'm 25 now, so 10 years, to just kind of sit back and reflect after losing everything that I had just worked for was probably the best thing I could have done. And taking that time, I was just kind of thinking where would be best for me."

Black said she was initially hesitant to sign with TNA because of its partnership with WWE. She was just coming off a WWE run and at first thought she wanted to be able to do something on her own without WWE.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

But after recalibrating, she said she believed TNA had the best environment for her and provided an opportunity for her to thrive.

"At first, I was a bit hesitant with TNA just because of the WWE partnership. And that's nothing against WWE at all. I loved my time there, but that that goes back to me saying I wanted to prove myself somewhere else and I kind of wanted to be as far away from WWE as possible at the time just to prove who I was without them because I was there for so many years or a few years before that," Black said. "At the end of the day, it seemed like TNA had the best environment. I wanted to help bring TNA to the forefront and help TNA grow.

"They got this AMC deal, and I was lucky enough to be a part of the AMC debut, which was great. But I'm not the one who built that. You know, I showed up on the debut. There were so many years of hard work put into getting TNA to the AMC debut and everyone there is a hard worker and everyone there loves wrestling, and it's very creatively free, which is something I think I wanted and needed to do to be able to challenge myself. Being in WWE for a long time, they kind of have what they want, and you obviously have input as well, but TNA is very, very open to ideas and everything like that. So, when I kind of thought about all that I was like, TNA is probably the place for me, which is great. I love TNA."

Black credited TNA with putting women’s wrestling at the forefront. Gone were the days of bra and panties matches or other risqué types of angles. TNA grew its Knockouts Division and helped make women’s wrestling more than just filler matches.

"I love the locker room. I think TNA has a great locker room environment in the women's division. Not only that, but the women put on one hell of a performance every single time," she told Fox News Digital. "I think a lot of the times, TNA maybe doesn't get the credit that it should for kind of being ahead of the times with the women, at least in my opinion. I think TNA was doing stuff that other companies weren't doing with women for a long time in the beginning. I remember watching TNA as a kid and even noticing that I was like, I want to do what the Knockouts are doing. They get to do way cooler stuff and everything like that.

"So, I think a lot of times maybe TNA doesn't get the credit for kind of putting the Knockouts on the forefront very early on and trying to show what they can do. So, that's something I've always wanted to be a part of. It's something I always noticed just even as a kid, that TNA puts a lot of trust and faith in the women, which is very great and something you don't always see. I think TNA is a great place and some place every women's wrestler should want to be because they truly believe in the women and I think the women are incredible, and we're all pushing each other to be better."

Black is now a contender for the TNA Knockouts World Championship. She’s one of the mainstays on "Impact" every Thursday night on AMC.

item.title: Vicious Vicki talks TNA Knockouts TV Championship tournament, why the promotion is where she wants to be
item.description: Vicious Vicki credits Tommy Dreamer for helping her earn a spot in the historic TNA Knockouts TV Championship tournament and why she wants to be with the company.
item.content:encoded:
Vicious Vicki has been a rising talent on the women’s wrestling scene, performing for Awesome Championship Wrestling (ACW) among other places over the last year.

While Vicki has been in the ACW women’s title picture, she recently was put into the tournament to crown the first ever Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) Knockouts TV champion. TNA introduced the belt at Slammiversary, and she was one of the independent talents who were selected to participate.

COMPLETE PRO WRESTLING COVERAGE ON FOX NEWS DIGITAL

She explained to Fox News Digital how she was able to get into the tournament after eventually losing to Indi Hartwell.

"I had a really great, still have a really great, relationship with Tommy Dreamer and a lot of the opportunities that I had had in TNA had come from him, and he made me earn them like I should have," Vicki said. "I do believe that he had some sort of part in that. But it was a road in TNA for me to, about three or four years ago, getting opportunities and just kind of taking the smaller ones, taking the smaller ones, taking the smaller ones, and then this was probably my first real, like my big match where I was able to kind of showcase myself a little more, which is awesome.

"No matter what happens, being able to be a part of history of TNA as an unsigned talent, … there was only two independent women's wrestlers in the entire world in that tournament mixed in with some WWE people. So that, to me, says a lot for me, and Gabby Forza was the other one. I feel like it says a lot that the work that we had put in over the years really paid off. So, no matter what happens, I can say that I was a part of TNA history and that can't be erased."

While TNA has received sharp criticism from fans wanting to predict that the death of the company is coming, Vicki suggested that couldn’t be further from the truth.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Vicki spoke to TNA’s resilience and being able to battle through the ebbs and the flows that come with any part of the pro wrestling industry.

"I think people just like to point the finger at everyone but themselves in life," she told Fox News Digital. "So, I just think a lot of companies, they flounder every now and then. It just happens, where there's highs, there's lows, there's highs, there's lows. It's just how it is. It's how wrestling is. It's been like that since the beginning of time.

"I think TNA will survive and they have survived much worse. So, I think right now, for me personally, that's where I want to be. Obviously, I would love to be walking down the ramp at WrestleMania, but you have to think realistically, and I think that's a great spot for me. I think they are doing great things. I think TNA is the spot. I do."

TNA airs "Impact" every Thursday night at 9 p.m. ET on AMC.

item.title: Pro wrestler BK Westbrook recalls AEW match against 'Speedball' Mike Bailey
item.description: BK Westbrook recalls his AEW "Collision" match against Mike Bailey and what it meant to finally wrestle on television after years on the indie scene.
item.content:encoded:
BK Westbrook is among the rising talents on the independent scene.

The North Carolina native has appeared in several different promotions since the end of 2025, including Game Changer Wrestling, Pandemonium: Pro Wrestling, Deadlock Pro Wrestling, TBD Wrestling among others. It’s not very often pro wrestlers moving through the indies get a chance to showcase their abilities on television.

COMPLETE PRO WRESTLING COVERAGE ON FOX NEWS DIGITAL

All Elite Wrestling made that happen.

Westbrook appeared on AEW "Collision" in May against "Speedball" Mike Bailey. He wrestled under just his last name and lasted for just over a minute with Bailey, who could be considered one of the top 10 pro wrestlers in the sport among any company. Westbrook recalled getting a chance to showcase his work with AEW in a recent interview with Fox News Digital.

"It was cool. So, I've done extra work a couple times. I've gotten to be kind of in the background, just somebody who's just happy to be a part of the show," he said. "But to be on the show and to be like wrestling, is my first opportunity that I got. Getting a chance to wrestle Mike Bailey, who's somebody I have history with from just being on various independent shows over the years before he's gotten this awesome AEW run and everything and being around him and like Deadlock Pro for instance.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"I know a good amount of people there at AEW so it was kind of cool to be in that environment. I was super thrilled just to be able to be on TV. I might not have been BK Westbrook, I might have been just ‘Westbrook,’ but the guy is still there. I was thankful for the opportunity. Absolutely. It was a good time."

Westbrook is set to reappear at Pandemonium Pro’s "JoJo" event in August. The show will raise money for the family of the late Jordan Saint, who was killed in a car crash.

Westbrook will go up against Epidemiyus Jr.

item.title: Hegseth turns tables on Dems as sparks fly during hearing clash: 'I put this on you'
item.description: Secretary of War Pete Hegseth clashed with Democratic senators over Iran war strategy and military funding amid a record $1.5 trillion budget request.
item.content:encoded:
Department of War Secretary Pete Hegseth blasted Democratic senators at a hearing on Tuesday, clashing forcefully with accusations that the United States had demonstrated a failure of leadership in its war with Iran, among other areas.

Sen. Gary Peters, D-Mich., raised skepticism with Hegseth, pressing him on whether the administration had a plan to bring the protracted war with Iran to a close.

Peters accused Hegseth of leaving the country in a worse position than when the war began.

PETE HEGSETH HEADS TO CAPITOL HILL TO DEFEND TRUMP'S IRAN WAR AS 60-DAY CONGRESSIONAL DEADLINE LOOMS

"You — you don’t have a strategy, you don’t have a long-term plan to actually win this war. Win the war! The men and women are going to be able to do it, but they need leadership. When will you show leadership?"

Despite promises to end the conflict with Iran in under 60 days and multiple attempts to broker a peace deal, the U.S. remains in a struggle with Tehran over control of the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway in the Middle East that acts as a bottleneck for much of the world’s oil trade.

The U.S. first began the war in joint strikes with Israel on Feb. 28 that killed Ayatollah Ali Kahmenei.

Hegseth fired back at Peters, pointing out that Democrats had allowed hesitations about the administration’s border enforcement policies to also extend to funding the government, including servicemembers.

IRAN WAR'S PRICE TAG HITS $80B — MORE THAN DOUBLE WHAT CONGRESS WAS TOLD

"When will you show leadership and stand up to your fellow Democrats who won’t fund the troops, who play partisan politics while you don’t give us the money? You want to stand up here and talk about the troops?" Hegseth asked.

"You won’t show any political courage to actually fund our department because you have Trump derangement syndrome, and you won’t commit to anything meaningful. So, I put this on you, senator."

As a part of his criticisms, Hegseth said he believes the hesitance Democrats have shown to the U.S. armed forces has stretched from before Trump’s time in office.

"I mean the Biden administration ignored hypersonics," Hegseth said, referring to missiles with the capability to travel faster than sound — a breakthrough China achieved in 2019.

"They just decided to pretend like that technology didn’t exist, and we shouldn’t invest in it. There are things — I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years. I really don’t. The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy; it was staggering when we took over," Hegseth said, referring to the Defense Secretary under President Joe Biden.

TRUMP SAYS IRAN DEAL ‘LARGELY NEGOTIATED’ AS 84-DAY WAR NEARS POSSIBLE END

The confrontation at the hearings comes as lawmakers weigh the Department of War's spending needs for 2027.

The administration has requested a record-breaking $1.5 trillion budget, a sizable increase over the roughly $900 billion Congress allocated for 2026.

item.title: Campus conflict exposed at the core of SJSU's messy fight vs Trump admin over volleyball scandal
item.description: Exclusive videos and documents reveal SJSU torn apart internally over the Blaire Fleming volleyball scandal as trans activists confront administrators.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE: Since the start of 2026, San Jose State University (SJSU) has had a donor ask to be removed from a key list in protest, a parade of pro-trans activists shout demands at administrators in an organized meeting, and assault allegations surface against its head volleyball coach.

Fox News Digital has obtained exclusive video and documents from independent verified sources, and emails via a public records request that reveal a university paralyzed and divided over its 2024 volleyball scandal involving Blaire Fleming and Brooke Slusser.

INSIDE THE FALLOUT OF THE SJSU VOLLEYBALL SCANDAL: ‘THIS IS AN OBVIOUS PROBLEM’

SJSU and the California State University system (CSU) are currently waging a lawsuit against President Donald Trump's administration to assert the school followed the law in its handling of the scandal, and to reject the findings of a federal investigation.

The lawsuit may set a national precedent for Title IX enforcement for public universities going forward, as SJSU faces internal and external friction.

On Feb. 26, a town hall was held in the SJSU engineering building.

A copy of an invitation obtained by Fox News Digital included the title "Trans Gender Rights Town Hall with SJSU Administration" in big, bold all-caps letters. The subject to be discussed was SJSU's response to the Trump administration's resolution proposal.

"Please come to a Town Hall with SJSU’s President and top administration, and tell them they must stand up for our transgender students," it wrote.

A sign-up form said that SJSU President Cynthia Teniente-Matson would be in attendance. But she was not there, as seen in footage of the meeting provided to Fox News Digital from an independent verified source.

Just two administrators were seen in attendance.

One by one, pro-trans activists came up to a lectern and unleashed emotionally charged rants, often directed at the two administrators. At one point, one of the speakers expressed being "glad" that the meeting was being recorded, so it could be sent to Teniente-Matson later. A separate video camera was seen recording the event, in the obtained footage.

Harsh orders were delivered to the two administrators there to tell their superiors not to accept the resolution agreement.

"It is about protecting all of the oppressed communities here on campus, doing more than lip service, and doing your job of going to the CSU and saying, 'We will not capitulate to violent transphobic demands or any other oppressive demands that come from this heinous, heinous current administration at all!'" one person exclaimed.

At one point in the meeting, a speaker declared, "It is your job to get in the room with Cynthia to make sure that she conveys all of this information to the relevant people. This isn’t a compromise. We’re talking about people’s human rights. We’re talking about enforcing segregation on our campus. That’s not something we can capitulate to. That’s not something negotiable."

Teniente-Matson's absence at the meeting was a frequent point of outrage for multiple speakers.

One of them shouted, "We heard from administration, ‘We’re doing our best. We’re doing what we can with the cards we’ve been given.’ Bull f—ing s—. Bulls—. No. Uh-uh ... Your best isn’t f—ing adequate. Like, just point blank. You can do the best. You can say it’s your best. It’s not adequate. People are harmed. People are scared."

Another cried, "45 minutes. They handed over 45 minutes for this issue, 45 minutes. And they didn’t even give us 45. They gave us zero because two of these seats are f—ing empty right now. They didn’t even show up. Gave a bulls— email about two hours before the event started."

Just eight days later, on March 6, SJSU and the entire CSU system announced they were suing the federal government to challenge the ED investigation and its findings.

In her announcement of the lawsuit, Teniente-Matson wrote, "Our support for the LGBTQ members of our community, who have experienced threats and harms over the last several years remains unwavering... We’ve heard the fear and anxiety that it has created and recognize that waiting for the university’s response has been difficult at a time already filled with uncertainty."

Administrator inboxes received responses from faculty, students and alumni, with reactions to the news, as seen in public records obtained by Fox News Digital.

"I attended the town hall and heard the students anxiety and pain," one professor wrote to Teniente-Matson. "Thank you for showing the nation how to fight with dignity."

Another SJSU employee sent an email to Vice President of University Advancement Judy Nagai, voicing concerns. That email was then flagged to other administrators.

"It’s worth noting that the university’s communications don’t directly address concerns about fairness and privacy for women," the email wrote. "It’s easy to see how the messaging might come across as ambiguous or even dismissive of women’s rights for some, especially given the strong emphasis on inclusivity for LGBTQ students."

Nagai forwarded that email to Teniente-Matson and other admins and wrote, "[redacted] replied with a lengthy response below. He will likely attend the alumni board meeting."

Nagai later sent out a mass email, advising admins on how to handle individuals with concerns about the lawsuit.

"If you receive any questions, comments, or concerns about this topic from alumni, volunteers, and donors, please connect with [Associate Vice President of Strategy & Campaigns Manaf Mansure] so he can track and assess an appropriate response with guidance from UMC and university leadership," Nagai wrote.

"This includes all forms of communication -- phone calls, emails, texts and mobile messages and in-person conversations."

A month before the town hall, and five days before the U.S. Department of Education (ED) announced it had determined SJSU violated Title IX, a donor sent an email to the school that was forwarded to Nagai. It was in response to an announcement about the school's dedicated philanthropy partner, The Tower Foundation.

The subject line read "no more giving to SJSU."

"Judy, please remove me from your list. I am totally against trans athletes playing in women’s sports. I am fine with trans people but the women who work so hard to advance their careers in athletics are getting robbed of their chances to rank and be recognized," the donor wrote.

"I was on the Tower Foundation for years and was a supporter. I wrote to the Chair and asked him to let the President know my feelings about this. A separate trans program is fine for me and makes sense. Title IX, in my view was to advance and protect women."

SJSU has addressed the donor's email in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"The University takes very seriously its legal obligations under Title IX. The University has followed and continues to follow the law," the statement wrote.

The email was provided to Fox News Digital in response to a public records request. The identity of the person who sent the email was redacted by SJSU.

Less than a week later on Jan. 28, ED announced that SJSU was found in violation of Title IX after conducting a nearly-year-long investigation into the volleyball scandal. ED gave SJSU an ultimatum to agree to resolve the violation that would have included an apology to players and changes to official program records, but no monetary fines.

But later that same day, SJSU’s Risk Manager Karen Vogler sent an email to a finance colleague, about a potentially different risk.

"It doesn't appear that SJSU will face any fines or monetary penalties as a result of the U.S. Department of Education's investigation. However, claims/litigation may increase given their findings that the Title IX violation goes back to 2022," Vogler wrote.

Over the last two weeks, Fox News Digital has reported on decades-old allegations against the coach who oversaw the controversial 2024 team. One of Kress's former players at Fairfield University sent a letter to SJSU in October 2024, near the height of the scandal's national exposure, with a letter that included allegations Kress attacked her in a hotel room in 1998.

The former player made her first public statements on Tuesday, since the allegations first surfaced on July 7 in a Fox News Digital report.

"SJSU has known I feared for the safety of the women in the program since October of 2024 and yet they have allowed Todd to continue to coach and travel with the team," she told Fox News Digital on the condition of anonymity. "I informed both Fairfield and SJSU of my 1998 encounter with Todd after reading accounts of young women on the 2024 SJSU team describing his intimidation, coercive, and manipulative behaviors."

SJSU is in this predicament three years after Slusser was allegedly convinced by Kress to move into an apartment with Fleming.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

The alleged treatment of Slusser, and every other player on those teams from 2022-24, would be codified in ED's 45-page findings were SJSU to agree to the resolution agreement.

But SJSU and CSU are seeking to have the entire investigation vacated, as stated in a copy of their lawsuit obtained by Fox News Digital.

CSU and the federal government filed a joint proposed order on March 27, 2026, that would prevent ED from using its findings to immediately disrupt federal funding.

Meanwhile, Slusser and former SJSU associate head coach Melissa Batie-Smoose continue to wage their own individual lawsuits against the institutions.

Slusser's lawsuit is set to soon receive a decision on motions to dismiss, after a federal judge delayed final ruling on the Title IX charges in the lawsuit until after the recent Supreme Court cases on trans athletes in women's sports. All nine justices in the cases, all nine justices unanimously agreed that federal Title IX law does not prohibit states from maintaining separate sports teams for biological females or from excluding transgender girls and women from women’s and girls' sports teams.

The unanimous interpretation of Title IX could prove to strengthen Slusser's chances of advancing past motions to dismiss, but does not guarantee it.

Meanwhile, Batie-Smoose is engaged in a wrongful termination lawsuit with SJSU.

Oral arguments for that case are scheduled for Aug. 21.

item.title: GOP gov claims swing-state opponent's agenda carries $18B price tag in explosive report as key midterm nears
item.description: Aaron Ford's policy proposals carry an estimated $18.1 billion price tag over four years, requiring billions in new taxes, a Lombardo report claims.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Nevada Gov. Joe Lombardo’s campaign estimates that Democratic rival Aaron Ford’s policy agenda would generate $18.1 billion in government and private-sector costs — burdening residents over four years — including nearly $8 billion in projected Nevada state spending — calculations Ford’s campaign sharply disputes.

The Lombardo campaign report assigns estimated costs to 76 Ford proposals, combining projected Nevada spending with costs it says would be borne by businesses and the federal government that ultimately fall to the taxpayers and the public. Ford’s campaign argues the analysis invents implementation plans Ford never proposed and misclassifies some federal and private costs as consequences of his platform.

The analysis projects $7.93 billion in direct state spending over a four-year term. Lombardo’s campaign argues Nevada could not absorb spending on that scale without higher taxes, new fees or cuts elsewhere, while Ford’s campaign says many of the projected expenses would not require new state appropriations.

In a statement to Fox News Digital, University of Nevada, Reno professor Mark Pingle backed the report's findings, saying, "Based on a review of Aaron Ford's policy proposals, an estimated cost of $18 billion is a reasonable assessment."

DEMOCRATIC SOCIALISTS HAVE LOTS OF IDEAS, BUT HOW ARE WE SUPPOSED TO PAY FOR THEM?

"The bottom line is simple: these proposals would require a record-breaking increase in spending. Before moving forward with a plan this expensive, Nevadans deserve clear answers about how it would be paid for, who would bear the cost, and what it would mean for the state's long-term financial stability. Regardless of political party, $18 billion in new spending deserves careful review and an explanation of how it would be paid for."

Because Nevada has no personal income tax, significant new recurring state spending would likely require additional revenue from other taxes or fees, reductions in other spending, or some combination of the two.

Ford's healthcare agenda, according to the report, carries the largest price tag at an estimated $4.25 billion for proposals addressing medical debt and predatory healthcare practices, including $3.5 billion for a proposal to cancel medical debt.

The report estimates an extra $1.76 billion would be needed to expand healthcare affordability and access through Medicaid protections, workforce expansion and preventive care.

ILLINOIS DEMOCRATIC GOVERNOR BUCKS TEACHERS' UNIONS IN SUPPORT FOR STATEWIDE CLASSROOM PHONE BAN

Ford's K-12 education plan is estimated to cost $3.68 billion alone, including raising Nevada's per-pupil funding to the national average, expanding pre-K, providing universal school meals, and increasing pay for teachers and support staff, the report projects.

The report estimates households would receive $17.88 billion in savings and other benefits while businesses, the state and the federal government would bear nearly the same amount in costs.

Overall, according to the report, Ford's platform would cost roughly $4.9 billion for education, $4.8 billion for housing, more than $6 billion for healthcare, about $1.5 billion for energy and nearly $900 million for economic development.

In a statement to Fox News Digital, Ford campaign spokesperson Prerna Jagadeesh said, "General Ford won’t be lectured by a governor who proposed a budget with a $335 million unconstitutional deficit and twice broke his pledge to never raise taxes."

Jagadeesh continued, "The Lombardo campaign is embarrassing themselves and insulting the intelligence of voters by pushing this fraudulent report. The report lies about Attorney General Ford’s policies and assigns fake costs to programs that won’t cost the state a penny and will actually save taxpayers money, and incorrectly attributes costs to the state. Lombardo’s campaign rushed out this incredibly sloppy report in a desperate attempt to divert attention from a viral video of Lombardo abusing his position of power to evade consequences for breaking the law. Nevadans already know Lombardo has spent the last four years raising costs on working families and is offering no solutions to the problems they’re facing."

The Ford campaign specifically disputes several of the report's largest assumptions, arguing Lombardo's team assigned costs to implementation measures Ford never proposed. Among other examples, the campaign says the report wrongly assumes Ford would spend hundreds of millions expanding the state’s Battle Born health plans and partially replacing federal Medicaid reductions. Ford’s campaign says his platform instead calls for outreach surrounding the existing Battle Born program and federal advocacy to prevent or reverse Medicaid cuts, not new state appropriations to backfill them.

The Ford campaign also showed Fox News Digital its own fiscal analysis, arguing the platform could be implemented without raising taxes and with substantially lower direct state costs than those projected by Lombardo's campaign.

Asked about Ford's contention that the report assigns costs to implementation measures not contained in his platform, the Lombardo campaign said it estimated what it would actually take to fulfill Ford's campaign promises, arguing that regulatory changes, enforcement initiatives and expanded benefits all require staffing, oversight or funding even when those costs are not explicitly identified by the campaign.

Former Republican Nevada state Sen. Ben Kieckhefer, who previously served as chief of staff to Lombardo, told Fox News Digital that Ford's proposals would require significant tax increases, saying the level of spending "is simply not sustainable for Nevada."

WATCH: DR. OZ SAYS NEWSOM, OTHER BLUE STATES HAVE TURNED MEDICAID FRAUD INTO A 'FEATURE'

"There is no realistic way to absorb costs of that scale without significant tax increases," he said. "Regardless of political party, proposals of this magnitude deserve serious scrutiny because Nevadans expect responsible budgeting, fiscal discipline, and a clear explanation of how these promises would actually be paid for."

Nevada Democratic lawmaker Daniele Monroe-Moreno, chair of the Assembly Ways and Means Committee, told Fox News Digital the Lombardo report "uses fake math to lie about Attorney General Ford’s policy positions" and said Ford's platform consists of "common-sense policies that will save Nevadans money at very little to no cost to the state."

The attorney general has also faced mounting questions over his spending during his tenure.

In March, state records reviewed by Fox News Digital found Ford racked up more than $410,000 in travel costs since taking office in 2019, while local outlets reported he spent more than 100 days outside Nevada. A spokesperson for the attorney general's office said the trips were tied to Ford's official duties and campaign activities and were permitted under Nevada law.

Ford is currently under investigation by the Nevada Commission on Ethics over allegations he accepted improper gifts or benefits and used his office to improperly benefit himself, according to documents obtained by local outlets.

Lombardo is seeking a second term and will face Ford in November in one of the most closely watched gubernatorial races of the midterm elections.
